please tell me if this lighthouse report is valid and what we can do to patch it? 

the json file is in ~/Downloads

I’ll inspect the Lighthouse JSON in `~/Downloads`, then map its findings back to the repo so I can distinguish real app issues from audit noise or local-run artifacts. After that I’ll either patch the clear fixes or give you a concrete patch plan if the report points at behavior-sensitive changes.

I found one likely report: `/home/adam-blumoff/Downloads/scopevcs.com-20260629T195450.json`. The repo also has unrelated modified Rust files already present, so I’ll leave those alone unless they turn out to be directly involved.

jq -r '.audits | to_entries[] | select(.value.score != null and .value.score < 1) | [.key, (.value.score|tostring), .value.scoreDisplayMode, .value.title, (.value.displayValue // "")] | @tsv' /home/adam-blumoff/Downloads/scopevcs.com-20260629T195450.json | head -100
find web -maxdepth 3 -type f | sort | sed 's#^#/#' | head -200

content.text: The report itself looks structurally valid: Lighthouse 13.0.2, run against `https://scopevcs.com/repos/adamblumoff/scope-vcs` at `2026-06-30T00:54:50Z`. Scores are basically green except Accessibility `0.96` and Best Practices `0.81`; I’m drilling into the exact failing audit rows now.

So far I’d classify the findings this way: the unnamed header link is valid and directly patchable; the unminified JS warning is not an app issue because it came from a `chrome-extension://` content script; unused JS is directionally valid but low priority because performance is already 100; the compression warning looks valid for the deployed document response. I’m checking the web app and deployment config to see where those fixes belong.

There’s a straightforward accessibility bug in `web/src/components/app-header.tsx`: when the breadcrumb is visible on small screens, the home link contains only the logo icon, and that icon is hidden from accessible naming. I’m also checking the API CORS layer because the deprecation is probably from `Access-Control-Allow-Headers: *` rather than Lighthouse itself.

input.cmd: jq '.audits["network-requests"].details.items[] | select((.url|test("scopevcs.com|clerk.scopevcs.com")) or (.mimeType//""|test("html|javascript|json"))) | {url, resourceType, statusCode, transferSize, resourceSize, mimeType, protocol}' /home/adam-blumoff/Downloads/scopevcs.com-20260629T195450.json | head -200

content.text: The CORS warning maps to `CorsLayer::permissive()` in the API. That sends permissive wildcard CORS headers, which is convenient but trips Chrome’s Authorization-header deprecation warning and is weak policy. I’m checking the exact Tower HTTP API so the patch can name the allowed headers explicitly instead of guessing.

input.cmd: rg -n "SCOPE_WEB|WEB|ORIGIN|PUBLIC|CLERK|API" README.md api/src web/src web/.env.local web/*.json -S

content.text: Compression probably belongs in the web server output, not the API: the document response was `text/html` and about 208 KB transferred, with Lighthouse estimating 136 KB of wasted bytes. Nitro has a build option for precompressed public assets, but that alone may not cover SSR HTML, so I’m checking whether this app can solve it in-process or whether it needs a CDN/proxy setting.

input.cmd: rg -n "compression|gzip|brotli|compress" web/node_modules/nitro/dist web/node_modules/h3 web/node_modules/srvx web/node_modules/@tanstack -S | head -200

content.text: I also verified the report was run with browser extensions enabled: the minification failure is literally a `chrome-extension://.../contentscript.js`, so that part should be ignored and rerun in an extension-free context if we want a clean score. I’m going to patch the app-owned issues now: header link naming and API CORS headers.

One important nuance: the document is huge partly because the TanStack Start config is inlining CSS into the HTML. Even if Railway is not compressing dynamic SSR responses, stopping CSS inlining should shrink the HTML response substantially and move the bytes into cacheable static assets. I’m going to patch that too, with Nitro precompression for public assets.

input.cmd: curl -sS -X OPTIONS -H 'Origin: https://scopevcs.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization' -D - -o /dev/null 'https://api.scopevcs.com/v1/repos' | sed -n '1,80p'
rg -n "inlineCss|compressPublicAssets|tanstackStart\(|nitro\(" web/node_modules/@tanstack web/node_modules/nitro web/src web/vite.config.ts -S | head -200

content.text: Live headers confirm the compression finding: the HTML response has no `Content-Encoding` even when requested with `Accept-Encoding: br,gzip`, and the response body is 128 KB. That makes this a real deploy/runtime issue, not just a stale JSON artifact.

The CORS deprecation is coming from the deployed API host in the report: `scope-api-production-0251.up.railway.app` preflight for `/v1/repos/.../events`. So the API CORS patch is directly relevant to the report.

input.cmd: curl -sS -X OPTIONS -H 'Origin: https://scopevcs.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization' -D - -o /dev/null 'https://scope-api-production-0251.up.railway.app/v1/repos/adamblumoff/scope-vcs/events' | sed -n '1,80p'

p='/home/adam-blumoff/Downloads/scopevcs.com-20260629T195450.json'

for k,v in data['audits'].items():
    if isinstance(v,dict) and ('cors' in k.lower() or 'header' in k.lower() or 'cross' in k.lower()):

content.text: I’m making three narrow changes: accessible name on the logo home link, explicit API CORS methods/headers, and web build settings that stop inlining CSS into the SSR document while precompressing public assets. These are all transport/UI fixes; they don’t change domain behavior.
I’m adding a regression test for the CORS header specifically, because that warning is easy to accidentally reintroduce by reaching for a permissive helper again.

# GitHub

## Overview

Use this skill as the umbrella entrypoint for general GitHub work in this plugin. It should decide whether the task stays in repo and PR triage or should be handed off to a more specific review, CI, or publish workflow.

This plugin is intentionally hybrid:

- Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for repository, issue, pull request, comment, label, reaction, and PR creation workflows.
- Use local `git` and `gh` only when the connector does not cover the job well, especially for current-branch PR discovery, branch creation, commit and push, `gh auth status`, and GitHub Actions log inspection.
- Keep connector state and local checkout context aligned. If the request is about the current branch, resolve the local repo and branch before acting.

Once the intent is clear, route to the specialist skill immediately and do not keep broad GitHub triage in scope longer than needed.

## Connector-First Responsibilities

Handle these directly in this skill when the request does not need a narrower specialist workflow:

- repository orientation once the repo, PR, issue, or local checkout is identified
- recent PR or issue triage
- PR metadata summaries
- PR patch inspection
- PR comments, labels, and reactions
- issue lookup and summarization
- PR creation after a branch is already pushed

Prefer the GitHub app from this plugin for those flows because it provides structured PR, issue, and review-adjacent data without depending on a local checkout. If the repository is not already identifiable from the user request or local git context, ask for the repo instead of pretending there is a repo-search flow that may not exist.

## Routing Rules

1. Resolve the operating context first:
   - If the user provides a repository, PR number, issue number, or URL, use that.
   - If the request is about "this branch" or "the current PR", resolve local git context and use `gh` only as needed to discover the branch PR.
   - If the repository is still ambiguous after local inspection, ask for the repo identifier.
2. Classify the request before taking action:
   - `repo or PR triage`: summarize PRs, issues, patches, comments, labels, reactions, or repository state
   - `review follow-up`: unresolved review threads, requested changes, or inline review feedback
   - `CI debugging`: failing checks, Actions logs, or CI root-cause analysis
   - `publish changes`: create or switch branches, stage changes, commit, push, and open a draft PR
3. Route to the specialist skill as soon as the category is clear:
   - Review comments and requested changes: `../gh-address-comments/SKILL.md`
   - Failing GitHub Actions checks: `../gh-fix-ci/SKILL.md`
   - Commit, push, and open PR: `../yeet/SKILL.md`
4. Keep the hybrid model consistent after routing:
   - connector first for PR and issue data
   - local `git` and `gh` only for the specific gaps the connector does not cover

## Default Workflow

1. Resolve repository and item scope.
2. Gather structured PR or issue context through the GitHub app from this plugin.
3. Decide whether the task stays in connector-backed triage or needs a specialist skill.
4. Route immediately when the work becomes review follow-up, CI debugging, or publish workflow.
5. End with a clear summary of what was inspected, what changed, and what remains.

## Output Expectations

- For triage requests, return a concise summary of the repository, PR, or issue state and the next likely action.
- For mixed requests, tell the user which specialist path you are taking and why.
- For connector-backed write actions, restate the exact PR, issue, label, or reaction target before applying the change.
- Never imply that GitHub Actions logs are available through the connector alone. That remains a `gh` workflow.

## Examples

- "Use GitHub to summarize the open PRs in this repo and tell me what needs attention."
- "Help with this PR."
- "Review the latest comments on PR 482 and tell me what is actionable."
- "Debug the failing checks on this branch."
- "Commit these changes, push them, and open a draft PR."
